content.body: All matter exists in one of three states: solid, liquid, or gas. The state of a substance depends on the arrangement and movement of its particles and the forces between them.

content.title: Solids
content.body:
Particles are closely packed in a regular arrangement
Particles vibrate but do not move from place to place
Strong forces of attraction between particles
Fixed shape and fixed volume
Cannot be compressed
Example: ice, rock, wood, iron

content.title: Liquids
content.body:
Particles are close together but not in a regular pattern
Particles can move and slide past each other
Moderate forces of attraction
No fixed shape (takes the shape of the container)
Fixed volume
Cannot be compressed
Example: water, oil, mercury

content.title: Gases
content.body:
Particles are far apart and move randomly at high speed
Very weak forces of attraction between particles
No fixed shape and no fixed volume
Fills the entire container
Can be compressed easily
Example: oxygen, carbon dioxide, steam

Summary table:
Property | Solid | Liquid | Gas
Shape | Fixed | Not fixed | Not fixed
Volume | Fixed | Fixed | Not fixed
Particle spacing | Close | Close | Far apart
Compressibility | No | No | Yes

questions.question: Which state of matter has a fixed shape and fixed volume?
questions.explanation: Solids have fixed shape and fixed volume because their particles are tightly packed in a regular arrangement and cannot move freely.

questions.question: Which state of matter can be compressed?
questions.explanation: Gases can be compressed because their particles are far apart and there is lots of empty space between them.

questions.question: In which state are particles arranged in a regular pattern?
questions.explanation: Solid particles are arranged in a regular, ordered structure. This is why solids have a fixed shape.

questions.question: Which state takes the shape of its container but has a fixed volume?
questions.explanation: Liquids have no fixed shape (they flow to fill the container shape) but they do have a fixed volume.

questions.question: What type of movement do particles in a solid have?
questions.explanation: Solid particles do not move from place to place but vibrate (jiggle) about their fixed positions.

questions.question: Which state has the weakest forces between particles?
questions.explanation: Gas particles have very weak forces of attraction between them, which is why they move freely and spread out to fill any container.

questions.question: Steam is water in which state?
questions.explanation: Steam is water in the gaseous state. It forms when water boils and particles gain enough energy to escape the liquid as gas.

questions.question: Ice is water in which state?
questions.explanation: Ice is frozen water — water in the solid state. The particles are arranged in a regular crystal structure.

questions.question: Why do liquids flow?
options: Their particles can slide past each other
questions.answer: Their particles can slide past each other
questions.explanation: Liquid particles are close together but can move and slide past each other, which is why liquids can flow and take the shape of their container.

questions.question: Which property is the same for both solids and liquids?
questions.explanation: Both solids and liquids have a fixed volume. Only gases do not have a fixed volume.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Matter can change from one state to another when energy (heat) is added or removed. These changes of state are physical changes — the chemical composition of the substance does not change. For example, water, ice, and steam are all H₂O.

content.title: Changes of State and Their Names
content.body:
Solid → Liquid: MELTING (energy absorbed)
Liquid → Gas: EVAPORATION/BOILING (energy absorbed)
Gas → Liquid: CONDENSATION (energy released)
Liquid → Solid: FREEZING/SOLIDIFICATION (energy released)
Solid → Gas: SUBLIMATION (energy absorbed)
Gas → Solid: DEPOSITION (energy released)

Key temperatures for water:
Melting point: 0°C (ice → water)
Boiling point: 100°C (water → steam)

During melting and boiling, temperature stays constant even though heat is being added. This energy breaks the forces between particles.

content.title: Melting and Boiling Points
content.body:
Every pure substance has specific melting and boiling points:
Iron: melts at 1538°C, boils at 2861°C
Oxygen: melts at −219°C, boils at −183°C
Alcohol: melts at −114°C, boils at 78°C

Impurities LOWER the melting point and RAISE the boiling point of a substance. Adding salt to water raises its boiling point above 100°C.

Evaporation is different from boiling. Evaporation happens at any temperature from the surface of a liquid. Boiling happens throughout the liquid at the boiling point. This is why wet clothes dry on a warm day even without reaching 100°C.

questions.question: What is the change from liquid to gas called?
questions.explanation: The change from liquid to gas is called evaporation (at any temperature from the surface) or boiling (throughout the liquid at boiling point).

questions.question: What is the melting point of water (ice)?
questions.explanation: Ice melts at 0°C. Below 0°C water is solid (ice), above 0°C it is liquid.

questions.question: What is the change from gas to liquid called?
questions.explanation: Condensation is the change from gas to liquid. It happens when a gas cools and loses energy.

questions.question: During melting, what happens to the temperature?
questions.explanation: During melting (and boiling), the temperature remains constant even though heat is being added. The energy is used to break forces between particles.

questions.question: What is sublimation?
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the change from solid directly to gas without passing through the liquid state. Dry ice (solid CO₂) sublimates.

questions.question: When water freezes, does it absorb or release energy?
questions.explanation: Freezing (liquid to solid) releases energy to the surroundings. The particles lose kinetic energy and form bonds.

questions.question: Why do wet clothes dry on a warm day without reaching 100°C?
questions.explanation: Evaporation happens at any temperature from the surface of a liquid. Energetic particles escape as gas, drying the clothes.

questions.question: Adding salt to water will...
questions.explanation: Dissolved substances (impurities) raise the boiling point of water above 100°C. This is why salty water boils at slightly above 100°C.

questions.question: What type of change is melting?
questions.explanation: Melting is a physical change. The chemical composition doesn't change — ice and water are both H₂O.

questions.question: The boiling point of water is...
questions.explanation: Pure water boils at 100°C at normal atmospheric pressure. This is where water changes from liquid to gas (steam).

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: A pure substance contains only one type of particle (element or compound). A mixture contains two or more substances that are not chemically joined. The substances in a mixture can be separated by physical methods.

content.title: Types of Mixtures
content.body:
Solution — a homogeneous mixture where one substance dissolves in another
Example: salt water, sugar dissolved in water
Solute = what is dissolved (salt), Solvent = what dissolves it (water)

Suspension — a heterogeneous mixture where particles do not dissolve
Example: muddy water, flour in water

Colloid — particles dispersed but not settled
Example: milk, fog, smoke

content.title: Separation Techniques
content.body:
FILTRATION — separates an insoluble solid from a liquid
Example: sand from water

EVAPORATION — removes liquid to leave behind a dissolved solid
Example: getting salt from salt water

DISTILLATION — separates two liquids with different boiling points, or gets pure water from a solution

CHROMATOGRAPHY — separates mixtures of coloured substances
Example: separating ink dyes

MAGNETIC SEPARATION — uses a magnet to remove magnetic materials
Example: separating iron filings from sand

Solubility: the maximum mass of solute that dissolves in 100 g of solvent at a given temperature
A saturated solution cannot dissolve any more solute at that temperature
Solubility increases with temperature for most solids

content.title: Distillation in Detail
content.body:
Simple distillation separates a solvent from a solution:
1. Heat the solution — the solvent evaporates (becomes gas)
2. The gas passes through a condenser (cold tube)
3. The gas cools and condenses back to liquid
4. The pure solvent is collected
This is how pure water is obtained from sea water.

questions.question: What technique separates sand from water?
questions.explanation: Filtration separates an insoluble solid (sand) from a liquid (water) using filter paper.

questions.question: In a salt water solution, what is the solute?
questions.explanation: The solute is the substance that dissolves (salt). The solvent is what dissolves it (water).

questions.question: Which technique would you use to get pure water from sea water?
questions.explanation: Distillation heats the sea water, evaporates the water (not the salt), condenses it, and collects pure water.

questions.question: What does chromatography separate?
questions.explanation: Chromatography separates mixtures based on how fast different substances travel through a medium. It is commonly used to separate ink dyes.

questions.question: A saturated solution is one that...
options: Has dissolved the maximum possible amount of solute
questions.answer: Has dissolved the maximum possible amount of solute
questions.explanation: A saturated solution has dissolved the maximum amount of solute at that temperature. Adding more solute will not dissolve.

questions.question: Which separation technique uses a magnet?
questions.explanation: Magnetic separation uses a magnet to attract and remove magnetic materials (like iron filings) from non-magnetic materials (like sand).

questions.question: To obtain salt from salt water, which technique would leave solid salt behind?
questions.explanation: Evaporation heats the salt water, driving off the water as steam, and leaving the solid salt behind.

questions.question: Milk is an example of which type of mixture?
questions.explanation: Milk is a colloid — fat droplets are dispersed throughout water but do not dissolve or settle.

questions.question: What is the role of the condenser in distillation?
options: Cool the vapour back to liquid
questions.answer: Cool the vapour back to liquid
questions.explanation: The condenser is a tube surrounded by cold water. It cools the gas (vapour), causing it to condense back into a liquid.

questions.question: As temperature increases, what generally happens to solubility of solid solutes?
questions.explanation: For most solid solutes (like sugar and salt), solubility increases as temperature increases. More solid can dissolve in hot water than cold water.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Elements, compounds, and mixtures are three different ways matter can exist. Understanding the difference between them is fundamental to all of chemistry.

Element: contains only ONE type of atom
Examples: O₂ (oxygen), Fe (iron), C (carbon)

Compound: contains TWO OR MORE different atoms chemically joined
Examples: H₂O (water), CO₂ (carbon dioxide), NaCl (salt)

Mixture: contains two or more substances NOT chemically joined
Examples: air, sea water, soil

content.title: Reading Chemical Formulae
content.body:
H₂O — 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom
CO₂ — 1 carbon atom and 2 oxygen atoms
H₂SO₄ — 2 hydrogen, 1 sulphur, 4 oxygen atoms
NaCl — 1 sodium, 1 chlorine
CaCO₃ (calcium carbonate) — 1 Ca, 1 C, 3 O atoms

The small numbers (subscripts) tell you how many atoms of each element.

content.title: Differences Between Compounds and Mixtures
content.body:
Compound:
• Fixed composition (always same ratio)
• Properties differ from its elements
• Hard to separate (needs chemical reaction)
• Example: Water is ALWAYS H₂O

Mixture:
• Variable composition
• Properties are combination of its components
• Easy to separate (physical methods)
• Example: Air can have different amounts of each gas

Empirical vs Molecular formula:
Empirical formula: simplest ratio of atoms
Molecular formula: actual number of atoms

Example: Glucose
Molecular formula: C₆H₁₂O₆
Empirical formula: CH₂O (simplest ratio 1:2:1)

questions.question: Which of these is an element?
questions.explanation: Iron (Fe) contains only one type of atom — iron atoms. It is a pure element.

questions.question: How many atoms are in one molecule of H₂SO₄?
questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ has: 2 hydrogen + 1 sulphur + 4 oxygen = 7 atoms total.

questions.question: What makes a compound different from a mixture?
options: Compounds have more than one element
Compounds have atoms chemically bonded in fixed ratios
questions.answer: Compounds have atoms chemically bonded in fixed ratios
questions.explanation: In a compound, elements are chemically joined in fixed proportions. A mixture has no chemical bonds between its components.

questions.question: Which formula represents a compound?
questions.explanation: H₂O (water) contains two different elements (H and O) chemically bonded together, making it a compound.

questions.question: Air is an example of...
questions.explanation: Air is a mixture of gases (mainly nitrogen 78%, oxygen 21%, and small amounts of other gases) that are NOT chemically joined.

questions.question: How many oxygen atoms are in CaCO₃?
questions.explanation: CaCO₃ contains: 1 Ca, 1 C, and 3 O atoms. The subscript 3 next to O tells us there are 3 oxygen atoms.

questions.question: The properties of a compound...
options: Are the same as its elements
Are completely different from its elements
Are an average of its elements
questions.answer: Are completely different from its elements
questions.explanation: Compounds have completely different properties from their elements. Water (liquid) is made from hydrogen and oxygen (both gases).

questions.question: What is the empirical formula of C₆H₁₂O₆?
questions.explanation: The simplest ratio of C:H:O in C₆H₁₂O₆ is 6:12:6 = 1:2:1. So the empirical formula is CH₂O.

questions.question: Which is easier — separating a mixture or a compound?
questions.explanation: Mixtures can be separated by physical methods (filtration, evaporation, etc.). Compounds require chemical reactions to separate.

questions.question: Salt water is which type of matter?
questions.explanation: Salt water is a mixture of salt (NaCl) and water (H₂O). The two components are not chemically bonded and can be separated by evaporation.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of States of Matter. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply States of Matter concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of States of Matter builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on States of Matter. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within States of Matter. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on States of Matter include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in States of Matter. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of States of Matter. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply States of Matter concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of States of Matter builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on States of Matter. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2

Common examination questions on States of Matter include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in States of Matter. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
